skills_extracted.name: React.js
skills_extracted.resumeClaim: 4+ years, Redux, React Query, 10+ apps built

skills_extracted.name: PostgreSQL
skills_extracted.resumeClaim: Uses MySQL; no PostgreSQL specifically mentioned

react.label: Intermediate-Advanced
react.rationale: Alex demonstrated solid working knowledge of React hooks and Redux patterns. Struggled with advanced optimization (memo/useCallback tradeoffs) but understood component architecture well. State management answers were practical and experience-backed.
react.nextStep: Deep-dive into React performance profiling and learn useMemo/useCallback tradeoffs through the React docs and Josh Comeau's course

nodejs.label: Intermediate
nodejs.rationale: Good practical Express experience with real traffic. Lacked depth on event loop internals and stream handling. Error handling patterns were basic.
nodejs.nextStep: Study Node.js event loop mechanics with the libuv documentation and practice with streams

postgresql.label: Beginner
postgresql.rationale: Alex has relational database experience with MySQL but PostgreSQL-specific knowledge is limited. Could not discuss JSONB, CTE syntax, or window functions. Schema design instincts are reasonable but query optimization knowledge is surface-level.
postgresql.nextStep: Complete the PostgreSQL Tutorial (postgresqltutorial.com) and practice with EXPLAIN ANALYZE on real queries

aws.label: Novice
aws.rationale: Cloud Practitioner certification shows foundational awareness but no hands-on infrastructure experience. Could not speak to ECS task definitions, RDS parameter groups, or CloudWatch alarms. Heroku experience doesn't translate directly.
aws.nextStep: Build a complete deployment: containerize the ShopDash project with Docker, push to ECR, and deploy to ECS Fargate with an RDS backend

system_design.label: Beginner
system_design.rationale: Alex understands single-service patterns well but lacks distributed systems vocabulary. Could not discuss CAP theorem, consistent hashing, or database replication patterns. Answers were concrete but limited to monolithic architecture patterns.
system_design.nextStep: Read 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' (Kleppmann) and study the system design primer on GitHub

learning_plan.overallSummary: Alex is a capable mid-level engineer with strong React and Node.js foundations. The main blockers for this senior role are PostgreSQL depth (currently using MySQL), AWS infrastructure experience (certification only, no hands-on), and distributed systems design knowledge. With 10–14 weeks of focused learning, Alex could credibly compete for this role.

steps.title: Fundamentals & Syntax
steps.description: Core PostgreSQL syntax, data types, and what makes it different from MySQL
steps.practiceTask: Migrate your ShopDash MySQL schema to PostgreSQL. Use JSONB for flexible product metadata. Write equivalent queries and note differences.

steps.title: Query Optimization & Indexing
steps.description: EXPLAIN ANALYZE, B-tree vs GIN indexes, partial indexes, query planning
steps.practiceTask: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on 5 slow queries in ShopDash. Add indexes where appropriate and document the query plan improvements.

steps.title: Advanced Features
steps.description: CTEs, window functions, JSONB operations, full-text search

resources.name: Advanced SQL for Data Scientists (Mode Analytics)

steps.practiceTask: Rewrite your AutoReport analytics queries using window functions (LAG, LEAD, ROW_NUMBER). Add a full-text search feature to ShopDash products.

steps.title: Hands-On Foundations
steps.description: IAM, VPC, EC2, S3, RDS — build real infrastructure, not just theory

resources.name: AWS Skill Builder — Solutions Architect Learning Path

steps.practiceTask: Deploy ShopDash manually: EC2 + RDS PostgreSQL + S3 for assets. Configure security groups, IAM roles. Don't use Elastic Beanstalk — do it manually.

steps.title: Containers & ECS
steps.description: Docker, ECR, ECS Fargate, task definitions, service auto-scaling
steps.practiceTask: Containerize ShopDash with Docker, push image to ECR, deploy to ECS Fargate. Set up a CloudWatch dashboard with CPU/memory/request latency alarms.

steps.title: Distributed Systems Fundamentals
steps.description: CAP theorem, consistency models, replication, partitioning
steps.practiceTask: Design the ShopDash system for 10M daily users. Write a 1-page architecture doc covering: database choices, caching layer, CDN, background jobs, and how you'd handle Black Friday traffic spikes.

quickWins: TODAY: Install PostgreSQL locally and spend 1 hour converting your ShopDash schema from MySQL — the hands-on exposure is worth more than any tutorial
THIS WEEK: Create an AWS free tier account and deploy any app to EC2 manually (not Elastic Beanstalk) — this closes the 'no hands-on experience' gap immediately
THIS MONTH: Read the first 4 chapters of 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' and practice explaining the CAP theorem out loud — senior interviews almost always include this
